data.title: Stock selection is the way to go as markets experience continued volatility - Raymond James’s Larry Adam
data.description:
Stock selection is now critical for investors navigating heightened market volatility driven by Federal Reserve leadership changes and the midterm election cycle, according to Larry Adam, chief investment officer at Raymond James.

In an interview with CNBC, Adam pointed to historical precedent, noting that Fed chairmanship transitions have consistently triggered market turbulence—from Black Monday occurring within two months of Alan Greenspan taking office to the “Vomageddon” volatility spike on Powell’s first day.

Combined with the typical underperformance seen during midterm election years, Adam urged investors to be highly selective in their positioning.

Technology (VGT), (XLK), (IYW) stands out as Adam’s favored sector amid the uncertain backdrop. He cited the sector’s impressive 40% earnings growth and valuations trading below their 10-year averages, describing it as offering “the lowest PEG ratio that we’ve seen.”

Adam expressed particular caution around energy (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE) and consumer staples (XLP), (VDC), (IYK).

He predicted energy prices could decline 30%-40% to around $60 per barrel by year-end, calling this “a big headwind in front of that sector.”

Consumer staples (XLP) present a similarly unfavorable risk-reward profile, with valuations comparable to technology but earnings growth of just 6%—roughly one-seventh of what tech delivers.

When it comes to market capitalization, Adam favors large-cap stocks over small caps, emphasizing that “you get a lot more earnings growth in that sector.”

Large caps also benefit from superior technology exposure at 32% compared to just 13% for small caps (IJR), (IWM), (VB), while small caps carry heavier exposure to the energy sector he views negatively.

The strategist maintains a clear preference for U.S. equities over developed international markets.

“The U.S. economy is showing that it’s a lot more resilient,” Adam observed, pointing to weakness in luxury retail sales in Europe and Japan as evidence of diverging economic fortunes.

Policy divergence further supports Adam’s bullish U.S. outlook. While he expects the Federal Reserve to cut rates just once this year, he anticipates the Bank of Japan and European Central Bank will raise rates.

This combination of stronger fundamentals, superior earnings growth, and more accommodative monetary policy leads Adam to conclude that U.S. markets will outperform their international counterparts in the year ahead.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Prudential outlines Japan sales suspension's financial impact
data.description:
Prudential Financial (PRU) outlined the expected financial impact from the extended suspension of new sales at its Prudential Life Insurance Company of Japan ((POJ)) unit to investors late on Tuesday.

The company earlier reported that it's extending by 180 days the initial 90-day suspension of new sales at POJ as it works to restructure the unit's governance, sales practices, and oversight. Prudential (PRU) stock fell 1.7% in late Wednesday trading.

The life insurance and retirement account company expects a $525M-$575M impact on pretax adjusted operating income in 2026 and a $400M-$450M impact on 2027 pretax adjusted operating income. Those amounts include the $300M-$350M impact, disclosed in February, expected from the initial 90-day suspension.

The dollar amounts primarily reflect the annualized impact of surrenders and the suspension of new sales activity for most of 2026, as well as the cost of restarting new sales in 2027, Chairman, CEO, and President Andrew Sullivan said on the investor call.

"During the extended suspension period, our focus will be on sustained, measurable actions designed to improve customer outcomes," Sullivan said. "This includes strengthening POJ's oversight and governance, improving the transparency of sales activities, and enhancing protections around customer information."

The estimated financial impact on Prudential's Japan earnings is ~18%, and on Prudential Financial's (PRU) is ~8%, the company said. More than 90% of POJ's pretax adjusted operating income is driven by its in-force business, it emphasized.

As part of the process to restore trust with its customers, Prudential (PRU) has formed an independent third-party customer reimbursement committee. The company has funded the committee with $70M.

"We believe the underlying fundamentals of the franchise, its customer base, its capabilities, and our long-term market presence and commitment remain intact," Sullivan said.

Prudential's (PRU) businesses in Japan consist of Prudential Life Insurance of Japan ((POJ)), Gibraltar Life Insurance Company, and Prudential Gibraltar Life Insurance ((PGFL)). The suspension of new sales only applies to POJ. However, the company is undertaking a proactive review of Gibraltar, which has 3M customers. That unit has received 70 inquiries about potential reimbursements. "To be clear, those are inbound inquiries," Sullivan said. "They're not confirmed cases of misconduct."

data.title: Boeing targets $1B-$3B 2026 free cash flow as 737 output plans rise to 47 per month this summer
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Boeing (BA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Robert Ortberg (President, CEO & Director) said the company is “off to a really good start and headed in the right direction,” adding that Boeing is “building momentum from solid performance across all 3 of our businesses,” while remaining “squarely focused on safety and quality, disciplined execution and elevating operational performance.”
  • Quote (President, CEO & Director Robert Ortberg): “We are pleased with the progress so far and remain on plan for the newest members of the 737 MAX family to be certified later this year with deliveries expected to start in 2027.”
  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Jesus Malave): “Consolidated revenue was up 14% to $22.2 billion… Operating margin was 2%… The core loss per share of $0.20… Free cash flow was a usage of $1.5 billion in the quarter.”

Outlook

  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Malave): “We continue to expect positive free cash flow of $1 billion to $3 billion this year,” and “we assume the expected DOJ payment to occur in the second half of the year.”
  • Quote (President, CEO & Director Ortberg): “To be clear, the wiring issue will not affect our full year delivery goals or plans to increase production to 47 per month this summer.”
  • Compared with last quarter, management reiterated the same full-year free cash flow range and again framed 2026 as back-end loaded, while emphasizing execution on certifications (737-7/737-10 and 777-9) as key enablers.

Financial Results

  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Malave): “Backlog continued to grow and remains at an all-time high of $576 billion, including over 6,100 airplanes,” and “cash and marketable securities ended at $20.9 billion… Debt balance ended at $47.2 billion, down $6.9 billion in the quarter.”
  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Malave): “BCA delivered 143 airplanes… Revenue of $9.2 billion… Operating margin of negative 6.1%,” and he added the company “remain[s] on track to deliver 500 airplanes this year” on the 737 program.
  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Malave): “BDS… Revenue grew 21% to $7.6 billion… Operating margin… to 3.1%,” and he said BDS “support[s] steady progress towards high single-digit operating margins.”

Q&A

  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: asked about conflict impacts on deliveries, services, weapons, and free cash flow scenarios; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: “we have seen no impact so far,” while flagging jet fuel and flight hours as leading indicators and saying “I’m not too worried about it right now.”
  • Ronald Epstein, BofA Securities: pressed for deeper defense growth areas; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: pointed to budget line items including “$5 billion… for F-47” and “$4 billion” for KC-46 production increases, emphasizing “tighter underwriting”; Executive VP & CFO Malave: said growth this year is led by “the tanker program, the classified programs as well as missiles and weapons.”
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: asked about quarterly cash flow cadence and risks from progress-payment deferrals; Executive VP & CFO Malave: guided Q2 free cash flow as “an outflow… in the range of… low hundreds of millions of dollars,” and said there were no meaningful deferral requests impacting the outlook.
  • Douglas Harned, Bernstein: probed timing and constraints to 47 and 52 on 737 and Spirit integration; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: said 42 is stabilized, 47 is planned “by this summer,” and 52 depends on ramping the Everett North Line and supply chain performance as inventory burns down, adding: “Spirit has done fine.”
  • Seth Seifman, JPMorgan: questioned 787 supplier issues, seats, and financial trajectory; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: said he sees “no showstoppers” on seat certifications but acknowledged timing, and cited engine delivery recovery plans; Executive VP & CFO Malave: said a 787 “cost base extension… is a good thing.”
  • Peter Arment, Baird: asked what milestones to track on 777X certification and long-term rate; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: said “The next one will be TIA 4b,” and added, “We’re targeting 5 a month.”
  • Noah Poponak, Goldman Sachs: challenged longer-term free cash flow confidence and BDS margin trajectory; Executive VP & CFO Malave: “first things first, let’s get to 10,” then cited higher production rates, higher-priced backlog, and productivity, while also saying BDS margin could be “kind of… 3.5% for the year.”
  • John Godyn, Citi: asked to “level set” BCA margins; Executive VP & CFO Malave: said Q1 included a “onetime benefit,” but expects “progressive improvement sequentially” and “margins to turn positive mid next year.”
  • David Strauss, Wells Fargo: asked about Spirit cash drag into 2027 and 777X change incorporation scope; Executive VP & CFO Malave: said Spirit is “$1 billion of negative cash” in 2026 and “probably similar” in 2027, improving thereafter; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: said there are “roughly 30 777s” requiring change incorporation.
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: asked about China orders and order cadence; President, CEO & Director Ortberg: said it is “100% dependent on the U.S.-China negotiations,” adding, “it’s a big number.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, centering on geopolitical disruption, cash flow cadence, and certification/production constraints, including direct scenario framing (“if the conflict drags another 3 months, 6 months or 9 months?”).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and often confidence-anchored with caveats, using phrases such as “we have seen no impact so far,” “we remain on track,” and “first things first, let’s get to 10.”
  • Versus last quarter, questions shifted from tariff risk and normalized cash-bridge mechanics toward war-related uncertainty, near-term cash cadence, and concrete rate-break execution, while management remained consistent on free cash flow range and certification priorities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The free cash flow outlook was reiterated at $1 billion to $3 billion for 2026, consistent with last quarter’s guidance language about a first-half cash use and stronger second half.
  • The major new external variable in the current call was the Iran war; President, CEO & Director Ortberg emphasized no delivery impact so far and highlighted backlog flexibility and possible defense upside as operational tempo rises.
  • Discussion of BDS evolved from last quarter’s tanker-related charge and program-risk framing to emphasizing “no major EAC adjustments” (Ortberg) and positioning for increased defense budgets, while maintaining tighter underwriting standards.

Risks and Concerns

  • Quote (President, CEO & Director Ortberg): “While we are seeing some regional instability as a function of the Iran war,” and he flagged potential aftermarket sensitivity to fuel and flight-hour impacts.
  • Quote (President, CEO & Director Ortberg): “we did see some impact to deliveries… due to delays of premium seat certifications,” and management described actions including earlier partnering in development and contractual off-ramps to reduce delivery-delay exposure.
  • Quote (President, CEO & Director Ortberg): “we discussed a potential durability issue on the 777X engine,” while stating the company “remain[s] on track for schedule of first delivery in 2027.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a “clean quarter” with improved operational momentum, while keeping 2026 free cash flow expectations at $1 billion to $3 billion and laying out near-term execution priorities: sustaining 737 production at 42 per month while ramping to 47 this summer, working through 787 seat certification delays without changing the full-year delivery range, and advancing 737 and 777X certifications to support higher future rates and cash generation. They said geopolitical risk has not affected deliveries so far, and highlighted backlog flexibility, defense demand tailwinds, and balance sheet actions including debt reduction as key elements supporting the 2026 plan.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF Holdings ranked by Quant as Federal rescheduling advances
data.description:
President Trump will reschedule marijuana as a Schedule III drug under the Controlled Substances Act, a major move that would allow for greater research of the plant, make it easier for pharma companies to develop cannabis-based medicines, and reduce some legal penalties associated with marijuana possession.

Axios, citing an administration official, said the reclassification could come as soon as Wednesday.

Against this backdrop of evolving federal cannabis policy, the following list ranks the top holdings of the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF by Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, offering a data-driven look at which cannabis operators are best positioned across valuation, growth, profitability, and price momentum.

Verano Holdings Corp. (VRNO) tops the list with the highest Quant Rating of 3.60, earning it a Buy rating. The remaining holdings on the list carry Hold ratings, with TerrAscend Corp. (TSNDF) and Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF) coming in second and third place with ratings of 3.01 and 2.90, respectively. Curaleaf Holdings (CURLF), Green Thumb Industries (GTBIF), Cresco Labs (CRLBF), and Jushi Holdings (JUSHF) round out the list.

The Quant Rating system grades stocks on their relative performance across various critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered a bullish rating and any rating of 2.5 or below considered a bearish rating.

Here is the list:

1. Verano Holdings Corp. (VRNO), Quant Rating: 3.60

2. TerrAscend Corp. (TSNDF), Quant Rating: 3.01

3. Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF), Quant Rating: 2.90

4. Curaleaf Holdings, Inc. (CURLF), Quant Rating: 2.89

5. Green Thumb Industries Inc. (GTBIF), Quant Rating: 2.74

6. Cresco Labs Inc. (CRLBF), Quant Rating: 2.64

7. Jushi Holdings Inc. (JUSHF), Quant Rating: 2.59

data.title: Old National outlines 4%-6% 2026 loan growth while keeping full-year guidance unchanged
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old National Bancorp (ONB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Earlier today, Old National reported first quarter 2026 earnings that exceeded our internal expectations and analyst estimates." (CEO & Chairman James Ryan)

  • "We delivered robust loan growth, powered by continued strength in our core deposit franchise and disciplined funding management in a highly competitive market." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "We also acted decisively on capital returns, repurchasing shares during the quarter, including reducing Otto Bremer Trust position in Old National, and we intend to deploy the remaining authorization over the course of the program." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "This is not a time where we need acquisitions to achieve our objectives." (CEO & Chairman Ryan)

  • "We reported GAAP 1Q earnings per share of $0.59." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Excluding $0.02 of merger-related expenses and a noncash expense associated with the final distribution of a legacy First Midwest pension plan, adjusted earnings per share were $0.61." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Specifically, during the first quarter, we returned $151 million to shareholders." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Outlook

  • "Slide 16 includes our outlook for the full year 2026, which is unchanged from our prior guidance." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "We believe our current pipeline supports full year loan growth of 4% to 6% and based on the results of the first quarter, we suspect we may trend to the higher end of this range." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Our NII guidance remains unchanged, and our balance sheet remains neutrally positioned to short-term interest rates." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "In aggregate, you'll note that we expect full year results that yield 15% plus growth in earnings per share and again, feature positive operating leverage with peer-leading profitability, good growth in fees, controlled expenses and normalized credit." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Financial Results

  • "We reported GAAP 1Q earnings per share of $0.59." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Total loans grew 8% annualized from the last quarter, led by 16.9% annualized growth in C&I." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Total deposits increased 4.2% annualized, primarily driven by commercial and retail growth and partially offset by seasonally lower public funds balances." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Adjusted noninterest income ... was $122 million for the quarter, exceeding our guidance." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Adjusted noninterest expense was $354 million for the quarter." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "We reported a record low 46% adjusted efficiency ratio, and we have now realized 100% of the $111 million of annual run rate cost saves that were anticipated with Bremer." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.: "what gives you confidence in the guide and what are the major puts and takes you see?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we've got a more cooperative yield curve today than what we had on average for the first quarter" and "we've got $5.5 billion sitting in the pipeline, up 14% year-over-year."

  • Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi: "Why not be more aggressive on the shareholder buyback or return?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we've got $383 million left on this existing authorization" and "We would fully intend to use that through the end of that authorization in February."

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: "Has anything changed environmentally in your favor?" Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "we're leaning into go-to-market strategies" and "We've seen that really come together nicely this quarter."

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: "How do you view the interplay" between CET1 and TCE? CEO & Chairman Ryan: "we're just trying to figure out what's the right long-term view and not get caught up in today's whatever short-term window might be."

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: "what you're doing on the AI front"? CEO & Chairman Ryan: "We've got an AI center of excellence stood up" and "Probably the first 1 for us that we're going to dive deeper into is in risk management."

  • Jon Rau (for Jared Shaw), Barclays: leadership changes and hiring focus? Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran: "Primarily on the C&I side, when you think about asset-based lending and core C&I middle market banking."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral on net interest income visibility and capital return pace, pressing on the path to the NII guide and buybacks, including "the year started a little weaker than at least the market had expected" (Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.) and "Why not be more aggressive on the shareholder buyback or return?" (Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly using stabilizing language on margin and guidance, including "stable to improving is the right way to think about it" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran) and "nothing we are seeing changes our outlook" (CEO & Chairman Ryan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language stayed focused on organic growth and self-investment, while the Q1 discussion added more emphasis on proposed capital rule changes and related optionality, including "we would clearly expect a capital benefit under the proposed capital rule changes" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 reiterated the strategic stance against near-term deals more forcefully, with "This is not a time where we need acquisitions to achieve our objectives" (CEO & Chairman James Ryan), compared with Q4: "it's not a focus" for M&A (CEO & Chairman Ryan).

  • Capital return discussion evolved from Q4: "we plan to be more active than we were last year" (CEO & Chairman Ryan) to Q1’s clearer execution and remaining capacity, including "We repurchased 3.9 million shares during the current quarter" and "have $383 million remaining under our program" (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran).

  • Guidance posture was consistent, with Q1: "unchanged from our prior guidance" (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran) echoing Q4’s framing of full-year 2026 expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The quarter brought higher for longer rate outlook and continued industry uncertainty." (CEO & Chairman James Ryan)

  • "The deposit competition is intense, but rational." (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran)

  • "Criticized and classified loans increased $113 million this quarter as Bremer loans transitioned to Old National's asset quality framework." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

  • "Given the continued focus on loans to non-depository financial institutions, we'd again like to emphasize that our exposure is de minimis." (Senior Executive VP & CFO Moran)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a start that "reinforces our confidence in the full year plan" (CEO & Chairman James Ryan), while keeping full-year guidance intact and pointing to record pipelines and disciplined deposit pricing as key supports for "stable to improving" net interest income and margin (Senior Executive VP & CFO John Moran). Executives emphasized continued capital return through the existing buyback authorization, reiterated that acquisitions are not needed to hit objectives, and highlighted potential additional capital flexibility if proposed capital rules are finalized.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sportradar falls amid Muddy Waters short call (update)
data.description:
Update 3:05pm: Adds company response.

Sportradar (SRAD) plunged 23% amid a short report from Muddy Waters Research. 

Muddy Waters disclosed that it's short shares of Sportradar. The Muddy Waters report came after a short report from Callisto Research on Wednesday.

Sportradar responded to the short seller reports in a statement emailed to Seeking Alpha.

 "A short report issued today contains factual inaccuracies about the company, and we unequivocally challenge these assertions.

The report demonstrates a fundamental misunderstanding of our business and the industry and was authored by a short seller trying to erode shareholder value and profit from stock disruption.

Sportradar works exclusively with licensed operators, follows strict global compliance, and due diligence standards, and we stand by our independently audited financial statements, risk disclosures, and information provided to investors and regulators," Sportradar said in the statement.

Sportradar (SRAD) has a short interest of 4.9%.

Sportradar (SRAD) is set to release its Q1 results on May 6.

data.title: Teleflex gains on report PE firms teamed up for takeover offer (update)
data.description:
Update 2:53pm: Adds detail on offer price.

Teleflex (TFX) rose 9.7% after a report that CVC Capital Partners and GTCR  teamed up for a takeover offer for the medical equipment provider. 

The PE firms have been working together to consider a possible takeover for Teleflex (TFX), according to a  Bloomberg report late Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

One of the offers, possibly from the CVC and GTCR consortium, may have been for around $170 a share, according to traders, who cited a Betaville item circulating on Wednesday. 

It wasn't clear if Teleflex would be receptive to a takeout and there's no guarantee that an approach will lead to a transaction, according to the report. 

CVC, GTCR, and Teleflex declined to comment to Bloomberg.

A potential offer comes as activist investor Irenic Capital Management last month sent a letter to the medical device maker urging the company to explore strategic alternatives, including a potential buyout, and revamp its board.

data.title: Albemarle downgraded at Rothschild Redburn on lithium pricing lag
data.description:
Albemarle (ALB) down 2.2% in Wednesday's trading as Rothschild Redburn downgraded the lithium miner to Neutral from Buy with a $188 price target, saying a return to market surplus in 2027 will weigh on prices and erode the investment case for major producers.

Rothschild analyst Mazahir Mammadli said he expects a rollercoaster trajectory in Albemarle's (ALB) earnings due to spodumene pricing and joint venture income dynamics, and noted that pricing hits earnings with a three-month lag, meaning Albemarle's pricing gain likely will peak in Q3 if spot prices peak in Q2.

Central to Mammadli's bear case is a dynamic playing out in China, where export tax rebates for batteries and battery components are being phased out from 2027, and the analyst said the industry is responding with elevated production in 2026, pulling forward lithium demand, but argued that "a reversal of this overproduction next year will lead to lithium demand deceleration."

With supply ramping up across China, Argentina, and the U.S., the analyst said he expects lithium carbonate equivalent prices to fall to $15-$16/kg by year-end.

data.title: United Airlines cuts 2026 forecast on fuel costs, ranks only fifth among U.S. airlines by Quant
data.description:
United Airlines Holdings (UAL) traded lower after cutting its profit outlook amid the recent surge in jet fuel prices.

Despite being the carrier most in focus this earnings cycle, UAL ranks only fifth among U.S. passenger airline stocks on Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, sitting behind not just the two Buy-rated names, but also rivals Delta (DAL) and JetBlue (JBLU), each of which edges it out by a thin margin.

With a Hold rating of 3.09, UAL's quant profile reflects the tension between its strong operational execution and the overhang of elevated fuel costs and compressed forward earnings estimates.

Topping the Quant rankings are American Airlines (AAL) and Southwest Airlines (LUV), the only two carriers currently receiving bullish assessments with scores of 4.31 and 4.12, respectively. Delta (DAL) and JetBlue (JBLU) trail with Hold ratings of 3.12 and 3.10, just ahead of UAL's 3.09. SkyWest (SKYW) and Alaska Air Group (ALK) round out the list with scores of 2.95 and 2.90, edging closer to bearish territory.

Seeking Alpha's Quant system grades stocks based on valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability on a scale of 1 to 5. Scores above 3.5 indicate a bullish rating; 2.5 or below signals a bearish profile.

Here is the list:

  1. American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL), Quant Rating: 4.31

  2. Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV), Quant Rating: 4.12

  3. Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), Quant Rating: 3.12

  4. JetBlue Airways Corporation (JBLU), Quant Rating: 3.10

  5. United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL), Quant Rating: 3.09

  6. SkyWest, Inc. (SKYW), Quant Rating: 2.95

  7. Alaska Air Group, Inc. (ALK), Quant Rating: 2.90

data.title: Wells Fargo lent to MFS as Barclays pulled out - report
data.description:
Wells Fargo (WFC) entered into a loan agreement with a Market Financial Solutions-associated property portfolio days after UK bank Barclays (BCS) started freezing accounts of the property lender that was on the brink of collapse, according to a media report on Wednesday.

A company linked to MFS borrowed £143M ($193M) from Wells Fargo in late November, Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter and documents that its reporters viewed. Barclays (BCS), meanwhile, was repaid about the same amount, they said.

The new loan closed soon after Barclays began to freeze some of MFS's accounts, putting the property lender on a path for its eventual collapse, Bloomberg said. The company failed in February amid allegations of widespread wrongdoing.

Wells Fargo's (WFC)  £143 debt is among the more than £2B of loans tied to MFS. Creditors have alleged a £1.3B gap in its finances, and the UK's Financial Conduct Authority is investigating MFS's operations.

Even with the payment it received soon after the Wells Fargo transaction closed, Barclays (BCS) remains MFS's biggest lender. It is still owed ~£500M, Bloomberg said. Barclays CEO CS Venkatakrishnan expects its impairment for the MFS exposure will be "materially lower" than that amount, he said last month.

Meanwhile, Wells Fargo (WFC) added $174M of charge-offs in its commercial and industrial loans for Q1 due to a "single fraud-related loss" in the real estate finance category, Chief Financial Officer Mike Santomassimo said in the bank's earnings call earlier this month. He didn't name the company involved but described it as an "isolated event."

Others that were left holding MFS-related debt are Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) and  Apollo Global Management's (APO) Atlas SP unit.
